quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Thymine dimer"?

options.text: Germline mutations (in eggs/sperm) are passed to offspring; somatic mutations affect only the individual and are not inherited
A UV-induced covalent bond between adjacent thymine bases on the same strand; distorts the double helix and blocks replication
Changes in chromatin structure that affect gene accessibility: histone acetylation opens chromatin (activating), deacetylation compacts it (silencing)
The end product of a metabolic pathway inhibits an early enzyme in the pathway, preventing overproduction (e.g., isoleucine inhibiting threonine deaminase)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Thymine dimer" is defined as: A UV-induced covalent bond between adjacent thymine bases on the same strand; distorts the double helix and blocks replication. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Base excision repair (BER)"?

options.text: Differential survival and reproduction of individuals with favorable heritable traits in a given environment; the primary mechanism of adaptive evolution
A series of protein kinases that sequentially phosphorylate and activate each other, amplifying the signal at each step
A gene-editing system using guide RNA to direct the Cas9 nuclease to cut DNA at a precise location, enabling precise gene knockout, correction, or insertion
Repair of a single damaged base: DNA glycosylase removes the damaged base, AP endonuclease cuts the backbone, and polymerase fills the gap

quiz_questions.explanation: "Base excision repair (BER)" is defined as: Repair of a single damaged base: DNA glycosylase removes the damaged base, AP endonuclease cuts the backbone, and polymerase fills the gap. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Nucleotide excision repair (NER)"?

options.text: A group consisting of a common ancestor and ALL of its descendants; the fundamental unit in modern classification
A series of molecular events by which a signal on a cell's surface is converted to a specific cellular response
Regulatory molecules bind to a site other than the active site, changing the enzyme's conformation and activity; can be activating or inhibiting
Removal of a bulky DNA lesion (e.g., thymine dimer) by cutting a ~30-nucleotide segment and resynthesizing from the template strand

quiz_questions.explanation: "Nucleotide excision repair (NER)" is defined as: Removal of a bulky DNA lesion (e.g., thymine dimer) by cutting a ~30-nucleotide segment and resynthesizing from the template strand. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Mismatch repair"?

options.text: Each diploid organism has two alleles for each gene; these separate during meiosis so each gamete carries only one allele
Exchange of genetic material between homologous chromosomes during prophase I of meiosis; increases genetic variation
2pq; the proportion of heterozygous carriers of a recessive allele in a population, estimable from disease frequency (q²)
Post-replication correction of mismatched bases that escaped proofreading; involves enzymes that distinguish parent from daughter strand

quiz_questions.explanation: "Mismatch repair" is defined as: Post-replication correction of mismatched bases that escaped proofreading; involves enzymes that distinguish parent from daughter strand. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Mutagen"?

options.text: The enzyme that synthesizes ATP as H⁺ flows down its concentration gradient from the intermembrane space back to the matrix
Control points at G1, G2, and M where the cell evaluates conditions before proceeding; can halt the cycle if conditions are unfavorable
An agent that causes DNA mutations: chemical mutagens (base analogs, alkylating agents), radiation (UV, X-rays), or biological (transposons, viruses)
The end product of a metabolic pathway inhibits an early enzyme in the pathway, preventing overproduction (e.g., isoleucine inhibiting threonine deaminase)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Mutagen" is defined as: An agent that causes DNA mutations: chemical mutagens (base analogs, alkylating agents), radiation (UV, X-rays), or biological (transposons, viruses). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A UV-induced covalent bond between adjacent thymine bases on the same strand; distorts the double helix and blocks replication"?

options.text: Germline vs. somatic mutation
Pre-mRNA processing
Thymine dimer
Starch

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Thymine dimer".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Repair of a single damaged base: DNA glycosylase removes the damaged base, AP endonuclease cuts the backbone, and polymerase fills the gap"?

options.text: Base excision repair (BER)
Carrying capacity (K)
Ligand
Chitin

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Base excision repair (BER)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Removal of a bulky DNA lesion (e.g., thymine dimer) by cutting a ~30-nucleotide segment and resynthesizing from the template strand"?

options.text: Linked genes
Crossing over
Semiconservative replication
Nucleotide excision repair (NER)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Nucleotide excision repair (NER)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Post-replication correction of mismatched bases that escaped proofreading; involves enzymes that distinguish parent from daughter strand"?

options.text: Mismatch repair
Ribosome
Recombination frequency
Steroid

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Mismatch repair".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "An agent that causes DNA mutations: chemical mutagens (base analogs, alkylating agents), radiation (UV, X-rays), or biological (transposons, viruses)"?

options.text: Mutagen
Crossing over
Genetic map
Central dogma

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Mutagen".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about DNA Damage and Repair Mechanisms is accurate?

options.text: DNA Damage and Repair Mechanisms protect genomic integrity from various sources of damage including UV radiation (thymine dimers), reactive oxygen species (oxidized bases), and replication errors.
Phylogenetics reconstructs evolutionary relationships using shared derived characters (synapomorphies) organized in cladograms or phylogenetic trees.
In eukaryotes, pre-mRNA undergoes processing: 5' cap addition, 3' poly-A tail, and splicing to remove introns and join exons.
Sexual selection and artificial selection are special cases of selection that shape traits beyond survival.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes DNA Damage and Repair Mechanisms as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of DNA Damage and Repair Mechanisms?

options.text: Protein denaturation (loss of 3D shape from heat, pH, or chemical changes) destroys function, demonstrating that structure determines function—a central theme in AP Biology.

Non-Mendelian Inheritance includes patterns that do not follow simple dominant-recessive rules: incomplete dominance (blending phenotypes, e.g., red × white = pink in snapdragons), codominance (both alleles fully expressed, e.g., AB blood type),...
Repair mechanisms include base excision repair (BER) for damaged bases, nucleotide excision repair (NER) for bulky lesions, and mismatch repair for replication errors.
Saturated vs. unsaturated fatty acids differ in the presence of C=C double bonds, affecting fluidity and melting point.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes DNA Damage and Repair Mechanisms as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about DNA Damage and Repair Mechanisms?

options.text: Exponential growth (dN/dt = rN) occurs when resources are unlimited, producing J-shaped curves.
Photosystems II and I absorb photons, exciting electrons that pass through electron transport chains.
Evolution and Evidence establishes that populations change over time through multiple converging lines of evidence.
When damage is too severe, p53 activates apoptosis to prevent propagation of mutations that could lead to cancer.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes DNA Damage and Repair Mechanisms as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of DNA Damage and Repair Mechanisms?

options.text: Cell Structure covers the organization of prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells.
DNA Damage and Repair Mechanisms protect genomic integrity from various sources of damage including UV radiation (thymine dimers), reactive oxygen species (oxidized bases), and replication errors.
Population Ecology studies how populations grow, interact with their environment, and are regulated.
Triglycerides (fats and oils) store energy (9 kcal/g) through ester bonds between glycerol and three fatty acids.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes DNA Damage and Repair Mechanisms as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on DNA Damage and Repair Mechanisms?

options.text: Tumor suppressors (p53, Rb) and proto-oncogenes regulate these checkpoints.
Repair mechanisms include base excision repair (BER) for damaged bases, nucleotide excision repair (NER) for bulky lesions, and mismatch repair for replication errors.

The three stages—reception (ligand binding), transduction (signal relay/amplification through phosphorylation cascades), and response (gene expression, enzyme activation, or cell movement)—allow cells to respond to their environment.
Speciation occurs through geographic isolation (allopatric) or reproductive isolation within the same area (sympatric).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes DNA Damage and Repair Mechanisms as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of DNA Damage and Repair Mechanisms?

options.text: Yellowstone-to-Yukon Conservation Initiative creating wildlife corridors spanning 3,200 km to connect fragmented habitats for grizzly bears, wolves, and elk migration
Sunscreen effectiveness in preventing UV-induced thymine dimers, reducing the risk of skin cancer by protecting DNA from photodamage
ABO blood type compatibility in transfusions determined by codominant Iᴬ and Iᴮ alleles and recessive i allele (3 alleles, 6 genotypes, 4 phenotypes)
RT-PCR diagnostic tests detecting viral RNA (COVID-19) by reverse-transcribing RNA to DNA and amplifying specific sequences

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of DNA Damage and Repair Mechanisms.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of DNA Damage and Repair Mechanisms?

options.text: Firefly bioluminescence using ATP to power the oxidation of luciferin, converting chemical energy directly to light with remarkable efficiency
Lynch syndrome (hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer) caused by inherited mutations in mismatch repair genes, increasing cancer risk
RT-PCR diagnostic tests detecting viral RNA (COVID-19) by reverse-transcribing RNA to DNA and amplifying specific sequences
Ecosystem services valuation estimating that pollination by insects is worth $235-577 billion annually globally, providing economic justification for pollinator conservation

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of DNA Damage and Repair Mechanisms.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is DNA Damage and Repair Mechanisms applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Xeroderma pigmentosum caused by defective nucleotide excision repair, making patients extremely sensitive to UV radiation-induced DNA damage
Genetic rescue of the Florida panther: introducing 8 female Texas pumas in 1995 restored genetic diversity and tripled the population from ~25 to ~200 individuals
Antioxidant research examining whether dietary antioxidants (vitamins C, E) can reduce oxidative DNA damage from reactive oxygen species
C4 and CAM photosynthesis as evolutionary adaptations to minimize photorespiration in hot, dry environments (corn, sugarcane, cacti, pineapple)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of DNA Damage and Repair Mechanisms.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of DNA Damage and Repair Mechanisms?

options.text: Sunscreen effectiveness in preventing UV-induced thymine dimers, reducing the risk of skin cancer by protecting DNA from photodamage
Cancer as uncontrolled mitosis: mutations in proto-oncogenes and tumor suppressors enable cells to bypass cell cycle checkpoints
Colon cancer progression following the multistep model: APC loss → K-ras activation → DCC loss → p53 loss → metastatic cancer over 10-15 years
Targeted cancer therapy: Herceptin (trastuzumab) blocking the HER2 receptor in breast cancers that overexpress this oncogene protein

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of DNA Damage and Repair Mechanisms.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows DNA Damage and Repair Mechanisms in practice?

options.text: Exercise physiology: the Krebs cycle's rate increases during aerobic exercise as increased ATP consumption lowers ATP/ADP ratio, relieving allosteric inhibition
Lynch syndrome (hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer) caused by inherited mutations in mismatch repair genes, increasing cancer risk
Lac operon as a model for understanding antibiotic resistance gene regulation in bacteria, where environmental signals trigger expression of resistance genes
Biomagnification of mercury and DDT through food chains: top predators (eagles, tuna) accumulate the highest concentrations, explaining why large predatory fish have mercury advisories

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of DNA Damage and Repair Mechanisms.
